content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcal PnecrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
September 7,2004
Rafael Adame
381 Siesta Drive
Aptos, CA 95003
Re:
Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our FiIe No. L04-fii
Dear Mr. Adame:
This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').I Nothingin this
letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The FairPolitical Practices
Commission (the "Commission') does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice.
(In re Ogtesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)2
QUESTTON
As the assistant community development director and building official for the
City of Watsonville, would you have a conflict of interest under the provisions of the Act
in obtaining the necessary building perrnits for construction of a house on a vacant lot
that you own within the city limits?
CONCLUSION
You would have a conflict of interest in obtaining the necessary building permits
for construction of a house on a vacant lot that you own within the city limits if you use
your official position to influence the decision regarding the issuance of the building
permits.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califonria Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not "orfe. 
the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation I 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)


File No. I-A4-n5
Page No. 2
FACTS
You work for the City of Watsonville in the capacity of assistant community
development director and building ofificial. As part of your duties you supervise building
inspectors, fire inspectors, code enforcement officers, development engineering plan
check staffand you have oversight of an outside consultant who does all the California
building code plan check/review. You are also the assistant commtrnity development
director, who acts as the director in the director's absence. During times such as this, you
would be the chair to the planning commission and also the lead staffperson for your
deparEnent at city council meetings.
You have recently acquired a vacant lot through the exchange process of selling
another property. This vacant lot is within the City of Watsonville's limits. You wish to
construct a single-family home and an accessoryunit. When considering this investrnent
proposition, you had to considerthe extent of yourinvolvemen! and you have verified
that this project would meet all the prescriptive requirements of the city and thereforg
would not need to go before the planning commission or city council. Your intent would
be to meet all of the requirements and pay all the associated fees without rquesting any
special modifications of any kind. You would not be involvd in the permit request
process or the actual construction of the homes. Your sister would undertake the request
for permits and would be the contact person. She would also hire the conkactor who
would do all of the work and interact with the city during the constuction process.
You have also contacted certified/qualified California Building Code plan
checkers and building inspectors who you would recorlmend that the cityuse as a third
party review instead of the city inspectors and plan check consultants. You would pay the
additional cost for this having this done. This would insure that here would be no conflict
with the city's plan check consultants and building inspectors, since you supervise them
or manage the consultant's contract.
ANALYSIS
Pomrvrtn.r, Coxrr,rcr or INTEREST
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them.- (Section 8l00l@).)
Section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govenrmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest-
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifoing conflict of interest. (Regulation 1S700O).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
one or more of his or her financial interests.


File No. l-04-175
Page No. 3
Srnp 1: Ans YouaPunr,rc Orrrcrnr,?
As the assistant community development director and building official for the City
of Watsonville, you me a public official under the Act (section 82048). Consequently, you
may not make, participate in making or otherwise use your official position to influence
any decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any
economic interest you might have.
Srrr 2: Wt t You Br M,uoNc, Pmrrcrparnvc rN, oR INtrLTTENCrNG A
GovrnxurNTAL Drcrsrov?
A public official "makes a governmental decision," when the official ,"acting
within the authority of his or her office orposition: (1) Votes on a matter; (2) Appoints a
person; (3) Obligates or commits his or her agency to any courss of action; (4) Enters into
any contractual agreement on behalf ofhis orher agency; [or] (5) Determines not to act"
on any of the above actions, unless such determination is made because of his or her
financial interests. (Regulation I 87 02.1 (a).)
A public official "participates in making a govemmental decision" when acting
within the authority of his orherpositioq the official "[a]dvises ormakes
recommendations to the decisionmaker either directly orwithout significant intervening
substantive review, by: (l) Conducting research or making any investigation which
requires the exercise ofjudgment on thepart ofthe official and thepqpose of which is to
influence a govemmental decision...; or Q) [p]reparing orpresemting anyrqrort, analysis,
or opinion, orally, or in writing, which requires the exercise ofjudgment on thepart of the
official and the pu{pose of which is to influence a govemmental decision...." (Regulation
18702.2(b).)
Additionally, "[w]ith regmd to a governmental decision which is within orbefore
an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of his
or her agency, the official is atternpting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision il for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears
before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any mernber, fficer, employee or consultant of
the agency." (Regulation 18702.3(a).) @mphasis added.)
As the assistant community development director and building official with
supervisory authority over the building inspectors who would be inspecting the
construction of a house on property that you own, and issuing the building permits for the
construction, obviouslS you would be participating in makin& or otherwise using your
official position to influence a govemmental decision if you exercise your supervisory
control over, influence, or participate in any way in the outcome ofthe decision regarding
the permits.
You would also be using your ofEcial position to influence a govemmental decision
(absent some exception) if you appeared before the city council orplanning commission in
your official capacity, for the purpose of influencing a governmental decision with respect


FileNo. l-04-175
Page No. 4
to the issuance or denial of any building permits on the property.3 However, under
regulation 18702.4, if you appeared before the city council or planning commission in the
course of its prescribed functions to represent yourself as a member of the general public
on a matter relating solely to your personal interest as described in regulation
18702.4b)(1), you would not be making or participating in the making of a governmental
decision.
Regulation I 87 02.4b) states:
"(b) ... an official is not attempting to use his or her ofificial
position to influence a govemmental decision ... if the official:
(1) Appears in the same manner as any othermember of
the general public before an agexrcy in the course of its prescribed
govemmental function solely to represent himself or herself on a
matter which is related to his or her personal interests. An
official's 'personal interests' include, but are not limited to:
(A) An interest in real property which is wholly owned by
the official or members of his or her immediate family."
STTT 3: Do You IIanTT A PoTENflALLY DIsQITALITYING ECoNoMIC INTf,REsT?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the mearring of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on thepublic generallS on the official or
on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect inveshnent of $2,000 ormore (g s7103(a);reg.l}703.l(a)); or in
which he or she is a director, officer, partrrer, trustee, employee, orholds anyposition
ofmanagement ($ 87103(d); reg. 18703.1(b));
o
.
3 For example, you have indicated that you would recommend the city use as a third party review
[certain independent] certified/qualified California Building Code plan checkers and building inspectors
instead of the city inspectors ad plan check consultants who you supervise ormanagc. By doing so, you
would be'\rsing your official position to influence a governmental decision'' withia the meaning of the Act
and subject yourself to the Act's conflict-olinterest provisions.
a For purposes of section 87lO3,"indirect investnent or interest msans any inveshent or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the ofiicial's agents, spouse, and dependent children oyrn
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a lO-percent interest or greater."
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or morea ($ S7103(b); reg. lg703.Z);


o
a
a
File No. I-04-175
Page No. 5
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 ormore within 12 months prior to the
decision ($ 871 03(c); reg. 18703.3);
A public ofiicial has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (g 87103(e);
re9.18703.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including
those ofhis orher immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule
($ 87103; reg. 18703.5),
Because you are t}re owner of the property upon which the building permits would
be issued, and we presume the property is worth more than $2,000, you have an
economic interest in the real property which is the subject of the govemmental decision.
This factor would not change simply by having your sister apply for the permits or act as
the contact person.
Srrp 4: Is Trru Ecoxourc Inrrnrsr Drnncrr,y oR INDTRECTLy Irwor,vnn rN THE
GovrnNuENTAL Dncrsrov?
"In order to dete,r:nine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial eflect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectlyinvolved in the
governmental decision." (Regulation 18704(a).) For governmental decisions which
affect real property interests, the standards set forth in regulation 18704.2 apply.
Regulation I 87 0a.2@) states :
"(a) Real property in which apublic official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of
the following apply:
tt...1l
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuancq denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlernent
authorizing a specific use or uses of the real property in which the
official has an interest."
Since the governmental decision involves the issuance ofpermits on propert5r
which you own, your economic interest in the real property is directly involved.
Srer 5: Marunnr,rrySmxnaru
Once the degree of involvernent is determined, Step 5 ofthe conflict-of-interest
analysis addresses the applicable materiality standard. Regulation 18705.2(a) sets forth the
materiality standards for directly involved real property economic interests. Under regulation


File No. I-04-175
Page No. 6
18705.2(a)(1), "[t]he financial effect of a governmental decision on the real property is
presumed to be material."
Srnp 6: Rr,LsoxABLY Fonnsnuarr,r
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
merepossibility. (InreThorner(1975) I FPPCOps. 198.) Underthefactsyouhave
presented, there is a substantial likelihood that the issuance of permits for construction on
your property would have a financial effect on your real property economic interest.
Srgps 7 & 8: oPugltc Gnxrn ll,r,y, AND sl,uc.ql.l,y Rrqnnro pannclmuox,
EXCEPTIONS
You have not presented any facts to suggest that analysis of the final two steps of
the conflict of interest analysis, step 7 - the'"public generally'' or step 8 - the "legally
required participation" exceptions is necessary.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
By:
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
wJLjg

City of Watsonville, would you have a conflict of interest under the provisions of the Act
in obtaining the necessary building perrnits for construction of a house on a vacant lot
that you own within the city limits?

CONCLUSION: You would have a conflict of interest in obtaining the necessary building permits
for construction of a house on a vacant lot that you own within the city limits if you use
your official position to influence the decision regarding the issuance of the building
permits.

written advice. (Regulation I 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
